action-executor.ts ApiCallFn has data?: any (line 29) — the sole remaining explicit any type annotation in a function signature.
types.ts EventSource<any>[] (line 427) uses any as a generic type parameter — this is in a base class method and is semantically correct (event sources can produce any event type).
schemas.ts uses z.any() for stateStore and onToolCall (lines 75-76) — runtime objects that can't be validated with Zod schemas. These are documented with comments explaining why.
SessionState and V2SessionState use unknown instead of any for result, engagements, signalSnapshot, priceSnapshot, oracleSnapshot fields — positive improvement from prior review.
ActionExecutorContext.llm is now typed as LLMProvider | null (line 84) — previously flagged as 'any | null', now resolved.

SdkBridge interface uses inline import() types for return values (e.g., Promise<import('./types.js').ScanPost[]>) throughout chain query methods — while slightly verbose, these are accurate type annotations.

DemosRpcMethods interface (sdk-bridge.ts lines 58-100) provides typed surfaces for SDK methods that lack type exports, with optional method markers (?) for methods that may not exist on older SDK versions.

cross_module_architecture.dimension_character: The codebase has a well-designed two-layer architecture with a clean toolkit/business-logic boundary that is consistently enforced. The main structural weakness is the god-module pattern at src/types.ts (65 importers), a single import cycle between extensions.ts and calibrate-plugin.ts, and orphaned files in src/lib/ that represent incomplete restructuring. Dependencies generally flow downward (plugins -> types, tools -> toolkit types, pipeline -> sources), with the cycle being the notable exception.
cross_module_architecture.score_rationale: The toolkit boundary is a genuine architectural strength — it is enforced and meaningful. The 65-importer type hub is concerning but is type-only (no logic), limiting blast radius. The single import cycle is real but contained. Orphaned flat files in src/lib/ are a boundary hygiene issue. Overall, a developer would mostly trust dependency direction but would encounter the hub module as a recurring friction point. Score lands at 81, reflecting 'solid but uneven' — strong boundaries with identifiable hub and cycle issues.

high_level_elegance.dimension_character: The codebase has a strong top-level decomposition with clear domain-aligned directories (toolkit, plugins, adapters, actions, reactive, lib). The public API surfaces via barrel exports are well-organized and documented. The main structural weakness is the src/lib/ flat file zone where 13 files coexist with 8 structured subdirectories, and the orphaned reactive/ subsystem that persists without active consumers. A developer would find the high-level structure intuitive but encounter confusion in the lib/ flat zone.
high_level_elegance.score_rationale: Strong domain-aligned directory structure with accurate documentation and clean barrel exports pushes the score up. The orphaned reactive/ subsystem and src/lib/ flat file mess pull it down — a developer cannot tell active from dead code without checking import graphs. Overall this lands at 82.5: solid decomposition with identifiable ownership gaps in specific areas.

convention_outlier.dimension_character: The codebase demonstrates strong convention consistency. camelCase naming, named exports, kebab-case file naming, and plugin structural patterns are applied uniformly. The few outliers are explainable: barrel files naturally differ from their siblings, and toolkit/tools/ outliers (connect.ts, feed-parser.ts) have distinct roles that justify their pattern differences. The main convention drift is the coexistence of deprecated module-level constants with their replacement functions in sdk.ts.
convention_outlier.score_rationale: A developer can learn patterns in one area and trust they hold everywhere — naming, exports, file organization, and plugin structure are all consistent. The score is pulled slightly below 85 by the deprecated constant coexistence in sdk.ts and the minor behavioral protocol variations in event handlers. These are isolated rather than systemic, placing the score firmly in the 'solid' range at 84.3.

error_consistency.dimension_character: The codebase has a well-designed error system in the toolkit layer (DemosError + ToolResult<T>) but the pattern does not extend beyond toolkit/. The rest of the codebase uses three different error strategies (throw, return null, try-catch-log) without clear per-layer conventions. Plugins intentionally swallow errors to prevent loop crashes, but this means error context is systematically lost at the plugin boundary. The providers/ directory is the worst offender with four different strategies in sibling code.
error_consistency.score_rationale: The toolkit layer's DemosError/ToolResult pattern is genuinely strong — a developer can predict error behavior for all 10 tools. But outside toolkit/, a developer encounters unpredictable error behavior: auth.ts returns null without context, plugins swallow errors, providers mix all four strategies. Empty catch blocks in hive-codec.ts and chain-scanner.ts are particularly concerning for a chain-operations codebase. This places the score at 74.8 — the strong toolkit pattern prevents a lower score, but the inconsistency elsewhere creates real friction.

naming_quality.dimension_character: The codebase demonstrates consistently strong naming quality. Function names use domain-specific verbs (loadAuthCache, guardAndPublish, evaluateTransition, checkAndRecordWrite), type names are precise and descriptive (SurgicalCandidate, PublishGateDecision, DemosErrorCode), and constants document their threshold purpose. A developer reading this codebase can predict function behavior from names with high reliability. The naming vocabulary avoids generic verbs entirely in the export namespace, which is unusually disciplined.
naming_quality.score_rationale: A developer can read function names and correctly predict behavior in the vast majority of cases — this aligns with the 86-90 range ('names are mostly precise'). The few minor issues (generic parameter names, ApiCallFn return type) are isolated and don't undermine the overall naming discipline. The consistent use of domain vocabulary ('attestation', 'hive', 'surgical', 'calibrate', 'provenance') is a clear positive. Score at 86.4 reflects strong naming with small local imperfections.

abstraction_fitness.dimension_character: The codebase has remarkably low abstraction overhead — only 3 pass-through wrappers among 213 functions, zero delegation-heavy classes, and zero facade modules. The four one-implementation interfaces all serve genuine extensibility or isolation boundaries (SDK volatility, plugin contracts, test doubles). The main area of friction is interface_honesty: the SdkBridge interface is wide (15+ methods) and ActionExecutorContext carries many fields, though both are justified by their architectural roles.
abstraction_fitness.score_rationale: Abstraction fitness is strong — the near-zero wrapper rate and absence of ceremony-only patterns place this well above the 80 'solid but uneven' anchor. The wide SdkBridge and ActionExecutorContext surfaces prevent reaching exemplary territory, but they serve documented isolation and testability goals rather than being accidental bloat. Score reflects a codebase that earns its abstractions with minimal wasted indirection.

dependency_health.dimension_character: The codebase has an exceptionally lean dependency footprint — only 5 production dependencies, all serving distinct purposes with no overlap or bloat. There are no competing libraries, no heavy utility packages pulled in for light use, and no visible unused dependencies. The single import cycle is mitigated through dynamic imports and type-only references, making it a low-severity structural concern.
dependency_health.score_rationale: Dependency health is strong — 5 purposeful production deps with zero duplication places this well above the 80 anchor. The one import cycle prevents reaching exemplary territory, but its runtime mitigation (dynamic imports) limits real-world impact. Score reflects a deliberately minimal dependency strategy that serves developers well.

low_level_elegance.dimension_character: The codebase shows strong internal craft in its toolkit tools — consistent guard-check-execute-record patterns, balanced extraction with withToolWrapper, and precise early returns. low_level_elegance.score_rationale: Low-level elegance is slightly below the 80 'solid but uneven' anchor. The strengths are systemic (toolkit-wide patterns) while the issues are concentrated in a few files.

mid_level_elegance.dimension_character: Module handoffs are generally clean and predictable — the DemosSession handle provides a single explicit handoff point for toolkit tools, the SdkBridge interface isolates SDK volatility at one seam, and the extension hook system composes plugins without tangled back-and-forth calls. The main seam friction comes from two parallel publishing paths (toolkit/publish.ts vs actions/publish-pipeline.ts) that use different integration patterns and type definitions, creating boundary confusion for developers navigating the codebase.
mid_level_elegance.score_rationale: Mid-level elegance sits at the 80 'solid but uneven' anchor. The toolkit layer demonstrates strong handoff design with DemosSession, ToolResult, and SdkBridge as explicit, minimal boundary contracts. The extension hook orchestration reads as clean composition. However, the dual publish pipeline and the actions/ layer's divergent integration style (direct SDK vs bridge) create genuine seam friction that prevents a higher score.

test_strategy.dimension_character: The test suite is substantial (1943 tests, 130 files) with strong coverage of the toolkit layer and minimal snapshot overuse. TDD is enforced at the tooling level. The main gaps are in cross-module integration testing and a few critical modules lacking dedicated tests (attestation-policy.ts). The consistent SDK mocking pattern prevents NAPI crashes but creates moderate coupling between tests and SDK mock structure. Previously reported issues (orphaned tests, missing publish-pipeline tests) appear to have been resolved.
test_strategy.score_rationale: Test strategy sits slightly below the 80 anchor. The volume and coverage are strong — 130 test files, TDD enforcement, minimal snapshots, and comprehensive toolkit coverage. However, the absence of cross-subsystem integration tests (toolkit <-> actions, plugins <-> extensions), the attestation-policy coverage gap, and SDK mock coupling create enough unevenness to prevent a higher score. The previously reported issues being resolved is a positive signal of active test maintenance.

api_surface_coherence.dimension_character: The toolkit API surface is highly coherent. All tools share the same (session, opts) => ToolResult<T> signature, wrapped uniformly by withToolWrapper, with Zod schema validation as the entry gate. api_surface_coherence.score_rationale: A developer seeing one tool can accurately predict the shape of all others — this meets the 90-level anchor. The connect/disconnect throw-vs-result divergence and minor opts optionality inconsistency prevent a higher score, but these are documented design decisions rather than accidental drift. Score reflects strong coherence with minor documented exceptions.

authorization_consistency.dimension_character: Authorization is well-structured with clear boundaries: the toolkit layer enforces session-scoped auth through connect(), and the plugin layer uses ensureAuth() for API token management. Both layers apply their patterns consistently within their domains. The main gap is between the two layers — they use different auth mechanisms (session handle vs. cached token) without a unifying abstraction, though this reflects the intentional toolkit-vs-CLI boundary.
authorization_consistency.score_rationale: A developer working within either the toolkit or plugin layer would find auth patterns reliable and consistent — matching the 85+ anchor. The dual-paradigm nature across layers and the missing permissions check on the auth cache file prevent a higher score. The SSRF validation, URL allowlists, and Symbol-based token storage demonstrate security-conscious design.

ai_generated_debt.dimension_character: The codebase shows disciplined commenting practices — module-level docstrings consistently explain WHY and architectural constraints rather than restating code. The pass-through wrapper rate is very low (1.4%). The main AI-debt signals are concentrated in a few areas: constant-level comments in scoring.ts that border on restating, and proportionally large docstrings on small handler files. ai_generated_debt.score_rationale: A developer would mostly find comments purposeful and code direct, matching the 85-level anchor. incomplete_migration.dimension_character: The codebase has completed its major migrations cleanly: chain-first (API to on-chain), CLI guard migration, and JS-to-TS. issues.summary: sdk.ts exports deprecated module-level constants alongside their replacement getter functions

issues.summary: reactive/event-handlers/ siblings use different structural patterns — some use EventHandler/EventAction directly, others delegate to createSeverityHandler
issues.suggestion: This is a minor convention drift since the delegation is intentional DRY behavior. Document the two handler patterns (direct implementation vs. severity-handler delegation) with a brief comment in the directory or in one of the delegating handlers explaining why.

While functionally equivalent, a developer reading sibling files would see inconsistent structural patterns.

issues.summary: src/lib/sources/providers/ uses 4 different error strategies across sibling files
issues.suggestion: Establish a per-layer convention: provider adapter methods should return typed results (following the declarative engine's 'never throws' pattern), while internal helpers can throw. Document this convention in a comment at the top of providers/types.ts.
evidence: providers/ directory: try_catch (22), returns_null (18), throws (11), result_type (1). The declarative engine 'never throws from any adapter method — returns empty results on failure' per its docstring, while individual provider operations throw on specific conditions. fetchSource returns FetchSourceResult (ok/error). No consistent convention for which approach to use.

issues.summary: auth.ts returns null for all failure modes — no error context preserved
issues.suggestion: Return a discriminated union type: { status: 'ok', token: string } | { status: 'expired' } | { status: 'missing' } | { status: 'error', message: string }. This preserves context without changing the null-check pattern at call sites (callers check .status instead of nullness).
evidence: loadAuthCache() returns null for: file not found, JSON parse failure, expired token, wrong address, missing fields. ensureAuth returns null when API is unreachable. Callers cannot distinguish between 'no cached token', 'expired token', and 'auth challenge failed' — all get the same null.

issues.summary: Empty catch blocks in hive-codec.ts and chain-scanner.ts silently swallow decode errors
issues.suggestion: Add minimal logging to the empty catch blocks using the existing observe() pattern: observe('warning', 'Failed to decode HIVE payload', { source: 'hive-codec.ts', data: { inputLength } }). This preserves the 'return null on failure' semantics while adding observability.
evidence: hive-codec.ts has 3 empty catch blocks (lines ~62, 85, 101) with only '// Not valid base64' comments. chain-scanner.ts has 2 empty catch blocks (lines ~68, 113). In a chain-operations codebase handling real money, silently swallowing decode/parse errors is risky — corrupt data passes through as null without any observability.

issues.summary: Plugins systematically swallow errors — catch-log-return without propagation
issues.suggestion: Add a hookErrors array to the hook context (already present in BeforeSenseContext as hookErrors?) and populate it consistently in all plugin catch blocks. The session runner can then report aggregated plugin health after each phase.
evidence: 18 try_catch blocks across plugins/ — all follow the pattern: catch error, log via observe() or ctx.logger, return early. No plugin propagates errors to the session loop. While individually reasonable (plugins shouldn't crash the loop), this means the loop has no visibility into whether plugins are silently failing. signals-plugin.ts catches and observes; tips-plugin.ts catches but only logs.

issues.summary: ApiCallFn type alias uses 'any' in return type — data field is untyped
issues.suggestion: Type data as 'unknown' instead of 'any'. This preserves the generic nature while requiring callers to narrow the type before use.
evidence: Line 29: 'export type ApiCallFn = (path: string, token: string, opts?: { method?: string; body?: string }) => Promise<{ ok: boolean; status?: number; data?: any }>;' — the 'data?: any' leaks type-unsafety through the API call abstraction name. While this file is documented as orphaned, the pattern could be copied.

issues.summary: sdk.ts exports 'info()' as a logging function — name is too generic for a module-level export
issues.suggestion: Rename to 'logInfo' or 'agentInfo' to communicate that it's a prefixed logging function, not a data-retrieval function.
evidence: sdk.ts exports 'info' and 'warn' functions (lines ~320-327) that are agent-prefixed console loggers. 'info' as a name collides conceptually with Node's util.inspect, Winston's info level, and general 'information' semantics. Callers import { apiCall, info } from sdk.ts — 'info' doesn't communicate that it's an agent-scoped logger.

issues.summary: The actions/ layer bypasses toolkit's SdkBridge abstraction, using direct SDK imports and its own type definitions
issues.suggestion: Wire action-executor.ts to accept a SdkBridge instance rather than individual function types — this would align the actions layer with the toolkit's integration pattern and eliminate the parallel type definitions

action-executor.ts defines its own function type aliases (AttestAndPublishFn, TransferFn, ApiCallFn) that partially duplicate toolkit's SdkBridge methods
This layer was written before the toolkit extraction and has not been migrated to use the toolkit's boundary contracts

issues.summary: attestation-policy.ts (8 importers) has no dedicated test file — critical path for attestation type selection and high-sensitivity keyword matching

issues.summary: Auth cache file ~/.supercolony-auth.json has no permission enforcement unlike the wallet file
issues.suggestion: When writing the auth cache file, set mode 0o600 via writeFileSync options (mode: 0o600) or use open(path, 'w', 0o600) + fstat pattern matching the wallet file security model
evidence: connect.ts enforces mode-600 on wallet files and checks for symlinks, but auth.ts reads/writes ~/.supercolony-auth.json via readFileSync/writeFileSync without any permission checks — the cached auth token is readable by any local user

issues.summary: Several scoring.ts constant comments restate the obvious rather than adding domain insight
issues.suggestion: Remove or enrich the restating comments: SCORE_BASE needs no comment (the name is clear), SCORE_CONFIDENCE could note why confidence matters ('Agent must declare confidence level — mechanical check, not judgment'). Keep the SCORE_ATTESTATION comment which adds genuine insight about TLSN vs DAHR
evidence: '/** Every post gets base points */' above 'export const SCORE_BASE = 20' — the name already communicates this. Similarly '/** Confidence field set on post */' above SCORE_CONFIDENCE. Compare with the useful '/** DAHR attestation (sourceAttestations) present — TLSN proofs do NOT score */' which adds non-obvious domain context

issues.summary: incident-alert-handler and market-alert-handler are pure pass-through wrappers to createSeverityHandler
issues.suggestion: These are borderline — they serve as named entry points with domain-specific configuration. If they grow no additional logic, consider inlining the config objects into the handler registry that consumes them, eliminating two files. If they are expected to diverge, keep as-is.
evidence: createIncidentAlertHandler() does nothing but call createSeverityHandler({name: 'incident-alert', ...config}). createMarketAlertHandler() does the same. The docstrings on these wrappers add commentary but no behavior beyond configuration objects passed to the shared handler.

issues.summary: Deprecated persistSourceToRegistry function has zero callers and should be removed
issues.suggestion: Delete the persistSourceToRegistry function (lines 324-350 approximately) and its associated V1 YAML imports if they become unused. The V2 persistSourceToCatalog function immediately below is the canonical replacement.
evidence: persistSourceToRegistry is marked @deprecated with 'Use persistSourceToCatalog for V2 catalog persistence' but grep shows zero callers in the entire codebase — it is dead code from the V1-to-V2 catalog migration

issues.summary: Deprecated RPC_URL and SUPERCOLONY_API constants in sdk.ts have zero importers
issues.suggestion: Remove the two exported constants (lines 33-35). If external consumers might reference them, add a breaking-change note. The getter functions getRpcUrl()/getApiUrl() are the canonical replacements.
evidence: Both constants are marked @deprecated with 'Use getRpcUrl()/getApiUrl()' but no file in src/ imports them. They are exported dead code from before the credential-override migration.

issues.summary: src/lib/ root has 13 flat files (3904 LOC) mixing domain logic with infrastructure at low fan-in
issues.suggestion: Staged reorganization: (1) Group tips.ts + mentions.ts + predictions.ts into src/lib/engagement/ (social/community features), (2) Group spending-policy.ts + budget-tracker.ts into src/lib/budget/ (financial concerns), (3) Leave agent-config.ts, state.ts, and tlsn-playwright-bridge.ts at root as they are genuinely cross-cutting. Update imports with: find src -name '*.ts' -exec sed to fix import paths, then run npm test to validate.
evidence: avg_fan_in is 1.1 across these 13 files — most are imported by only 1-2 files
tips.ts, mentions.ts, predictions.ts are domain features that could group under a 'social/' or 'engagement/' subdirectory
spending-policy.ts, budget-tracker.ts are financial concerns that could group under 'budget/'

The existing subdirectories (attestation/, pipeline/, scoring/) show the project's preferred organization pattern

issues.summary: Deprecated RPC_URL/SUPERCOLONY_API constants still exported alongside getter functions in sdk.ts
issues.suggestion: Remove the deprecated constant exports entirely. Any remaining consumers should be migrated to getRpcUrl()/getApiUrl(). The @deprecated annotation has been in place — complete the migration by deleting the exports.
evidence: Lines 33-35: RPC_URL and SUPERCOLONY_API are exported as const values computed at module load time. getRpcUrl()/getApiUrl() return the dynamic runtimeConfig values. The constants don't reflect credential file overrides applied by loadMnemonic().

issues.summary: sdk.ts has 3 module-level mutable variables creating implicit boot-order coupling
issues.suggestion: Consider encapsulating runtimeConfig, cryptoInitialized, and logAgentName into a SdkContext object created by a factory function, similar to how sdk-bridge.ts uses closure-scoped state. This would make the initialization dependency explicit and support multiple concurrent agent configs in the same process.
evidence: runtimeConfig (line 30), cryptoInitialized (line 36), logAgentName (line 37) are module-level let variables. loadMnemonic() mutates runtimeConfig as a side effect, meaning the order of loadMnemonic() vs getRpcUrl() calls matters. Multiple callers of loadMnemonic() in the same process would overwrite each other's config.

issues.summary: sdk-bridge.ts at 917 lines mixes transport, serialization, chain queries, and payment logic
issues.suggestion: Extract chain-query methods (getHivePosts, getHiveReactions, getHivePostsByAuthor, getHiveReactionsByAuthor, getRepliesTo, verifyTransaction) into a dedicated chain-reader.ts module. The SdkBridge interface can compose the chain reader, keeping the factory pattern intact while reducing sdk-bridge.ts to ~500 lines of write-path logic.
evidence: Single file implements: DAHR attestation, API calls with retry, HIVE post encoding/publishing, DEM token transfers, D402 payment settlement, chain transaction queries (5 methods), reaction publishing, and transaction verification. These are at least 4 distinct responsibility clusters.

issues.summary: publish-pipeline.ts uses 7 'as any' casts for txHash extraction that sdk-bridge.ts handles with typed narrowing
issues.suggestion: Extract extractTxHash() to a shared utility (e.g., toolkit/tx-utils.ts) and use it in publish-pipeline.ts, replacing all 7 'as any' casts with the typed version.
evidence: Lines 342-350 use (validity as any)?.response?.data?.transaction?.hash and 6 more 'as any' casts. sdk-bridge.ts has extractTxHash() (line 222) that handles the same SDK response shapes with proper typed narrowing using Record<string, unknown> casts.

issues.summary: 6 files share smells+structural signals — large files with mixed complexity patterns
issues.suggestion: For sdk-bridge.ts, extract chain-reader methods as described above. For the other 5, no extraction is recommended — they are long but single-responsibility. Add section headers (already present in most) to aid navigation.
evidence: All 6 files are 500+ LOC and flagged by both smells and structural detectors. However, 5 of 6 (agent-config, signal-detection, catalog, matcher, declarative-engine) are long due to genuinely complex domains — validation logic, source matching, YAML spec processing. Only sdk-bridge.ts mixes distinct responsibilities.

issues.reasoning: File is part of intentionally retained reactive subsystem with active tests, previously adjudicated as false positive
Intentionally retained reactive subsystem code, previously adjudicated as false positive
21-field interface is intentional DI for testability — each field is a distinct dependency, not related params that should be grouped

issues.summary: apiCall<TData> in sdk.ts returns error message string as TData on network failure
issues.suggestion: Change ApiCallResult to use a discriminated union: { ok: true; status: number; data: TData } | { ok: false; status: number; data: string; errorType?: string }. This makes the type system enforce error checking before accessing TData-shaped data.
evidence: Line 298: on catch, returns { ok: false, status: 0, data: message as TData }. The data field is typed as TData (generic) but receives a plain string error message. Callers checking ok=false before accessing data are safe, but the type annotation lies about what data contains on error paths.

issues.summary: generatePost silently overrides invalid category/confidence/predicted_reactions without notifying caller
issues.suggestion: Add an optional warnings array to PostDraft (e.g., correctedFields: string[]) so callers can log or factor in that the LLM output was repaired. Alternatively, log these corrections via info() alongside the existing generation log message.
evidence: Lines 260-268: if category is invalid, it's silently replaced with input.category. If confidence is out of range, silently set to 70. If predicted_reactions is negative, silently set to 8. Caller receives a PostDraft with no indication that fields were corrected.

issues.summary: loadStrategyContext uses heuristic regex exit condition for YAML section extraction
issues.suggestion: Replace the regex heuristic with explicit YAML key detection: /^[a-z_]+:/ to match only top-level YAML keys. Add a comment explaining the intent: 'Stop when we reach the next top-level YAML section'.
evidence: Line 116-117: breaks section extraction when relevant.length > 30 AND (line is empty OR line matches /^[a-z]/). The /^[a-z]/ test is a proxy for 'new YAML top-level key' but would also match continuation lines that happen to start with lowercase. This works in practice because strategy.yaml follows a specific format, but the intent is not self-documenting.

issues.summary: 9-line txHash extraction in publish-pipeline.ts uses chained as-any casts obscuring logic
issues.suggestion: Import and use extractTxHash from sdk-bridge.ts (or a shared utility). This eliminates the as-any casts and makes the response shape handling self-documenting via the typed Record<string, unknown> narrowing.
evidence: Lines 342-350: 7 'as any' casts in a chained OR expression covering 5 different SDK response shapes. The logic is correct but hard to verify because the type system is bypassed. sdk-bridge.ts extractTxHash() (line 222) implements the same logic with typed narrowing.

issues.summary: publish-pipeline.ts has 7 'as any' casts in txHash extraction — typed alternative exists
issues.suggestion: Extract extractTxHash() to a shared utility and import in publish-pipeline.ts. Replace lines 342-350 with: const txHash = extractTxHash(validity, result);
evidence: Lines 342-350: (validity as any)?.response?.data?.transaction?.hash and 6 more 'as any' casts. sdk-bridge.ts extractTxHash() (line 222) implements identical logic with typed Record<string, unknown> narrowing.

issues.summary: ApiCallFn type alias has data?: any — should use unknown
issues.suggestion: Change data?: any to data?: unknown. Callers already use conditional checks before accessing data properties, so narrowing from unknown is compatible.
evidence: Line 29: Promise<{ ok: boolean; status?: number; data?: any }>. This is the only remaining explicit 'any' type annotation in a public function signature across all production code.

issues.summary: V2SessionState has 5 unknown-typed fields that consumers must unsafely narrow
issues.suggestion: Define specific types for signalSnapshot (SignalSnapshot from signals.ts), priceSnapshot, and oracleSnapshot based on their actual runtime shapes. For engagements, define an EngagementRecord type capturing the known fields. For result, use a union of phase-specific result types.
evidence: result?: unknown (line 52, 95), engagements: Record<string, unknown>[] (line 65, 132), signalSnapshot?: unknown (line 138), priceSnapshot?: unknown (line 140), oracleSnapshot?: unknown (line 142). These are correct over 'any' but force consumers to use unsafe casts when reading values.

add.description: src/toolkit/ never imports from src/lib/, src/actions/, or src/plugins/. This architectural boundary is consistently enforced and meaningful — toolkit remains framework-agnostic.

add.header: Type-only hub modules limit blast radius
add.description: The three type hubs (src/types.ts at 65, src/toolkit/types.ts at 23, src/adapters/skill-dojo/types.ts at 18) are all type-definition files with no runtime logic. Changes propagate at compile-time only, reducing the practical blast radius compared to logic hubs.

add.header: Consistent downward dependency flow in plugins
add.description: All 22 plugins import from src/types.ts and optionally from src/lib/ — none import from each other or from cli/. This enforces a clean unidirectional dependency graph.
Top-level directories (toolkit/, plugins/, adapters/, actions/, reactive/, lib/, cli/) each own a clear domain capability. A new engineer can predict directory purpose from the name alone.
docs/project-structure.md and CLAUDE.md accurately reflect the actual directory layout, file counts, and architectural boundaries. This is rare and valuable for developer onboarding.

add.header: Well-organized barrel exports with section comments
add.description: Both src/index.ts and src/toolkit/index.ts use descriptive section comments and explicit named exports, making the public API surface clear and navigable.

The restructuring from flat files to domain subdirectories in src/lib/ is partially done — 8 subdirectories exist but 13 flat files remain. This is a known debt item documented in project-structure.md.

add.header: Uniform camelCase naming across all directories
add.description: Every directory in the codebase uses camelCase for function and variable names with zero snake_case outliers. This eliminates a common source of convention drift in TypeScript projects.
All modules use named exports exclusively — no default exports anywhere. This makes imports predictable and grep-able across the entire codebase.
All source files use kebab-case naming (e.g., sdk-bridge.ts, publish-pipeline.ts, state-helpers.ts). No PascalCase or camelCase files exist.
21 of 22 plugins follow the same structural pattern with consistent type imports. The sole outlier is the barrel index.ts which naturally differs.

add.header: Strong typed error system in toolkit layer
add.description: DemosError with 10 typed error codes, retryable flag, and ToolResult<T> envelope. withToolWrapper provides consistent error wrapping for all 10 tools. A developer can predict error behavior for any toolkit call.

connect.ts and disconnect.ts use throw semantics instead of ToolResult — documented as intentional because they manage session lifecycle, not tool operations. This is a deliberate layer-appropriate strategy.

add.header: Plugin error-swallowing is intentional but lacks observability
add.description: Plugins catch and log errors to prevent crashing the session loop. This is a deliberate design choice, but the lack of aggregated error reporting means plugin health is invisible to the session runner.

add.header: Four error strategies without per-layer convention
add.description: toolkit/ uses ToolResult, plugins/ use try-catch-swallow, lib/ mixes throw/null/try-catch, providers/ uses all four. No documented convention exists for which strategy to use in which layer.

The export namespace avoids generic verbs (process, handle, do, manage) entirely. Function names use specific verbs: loadAuthCache, guardAndPublish, evaluateTransition, checkAndRecordWrite, acquireRateLimitToken. This discipline makes the codebase navigable by name alone.

Types like SurgicalCandidate, PublishGateDecision, SourceMatchDecision, DemosErrorCode, ToolResult<T>, and ProvenancePath communicate their domain role without ambiguity. A developer can understand the type system from names alone.

Numeric constants are named for their domain meaning: PROMOTION_PASSES, DEGRADED_RATING_THRESHOLD, MIN_REACTIONS_FOR_ENGAGEMENT, QUARANTINE_ARCHIVE_FAILURES. No magic numbers without names in the production code.
The 'ctx' abbreviation for hook context parameters is used 50+ times across all plugins and extensions. This is a project convention, not a naming outlier.

Only 3 of 213 function signatures are pass-through wrappers (0.014 rate). The three wrappers (calibrate-plugin, incident-alert-handler, market-alert-handler) all add declarative config mapping rather than blind forwarding.
All 4 one-implementation interfaces (ElizaService, FrameworkPlugin, LLMProvider, StateStore) serve genuine extensibility or SDK isolation boundaries with documented design rationale, not ceremony.

add.header: Tool wrapper adds real cross-cutting behavior
add.description: withToolWrapper adds timing, session touch, observer notification, and typed error wrapping — genuine cross-cutting concerns, not pass-through indirection.

add.header: SdkBridge is intentionally wide for SDK isolation
add.description: The wide SdkBridge interface (15+ methods) is the primary SDK volatility isolation layer. All toolkit tools interact with the chain through this seam, making it a deliberate architectural boundary rather than accidental bloat.
Only 5 production dependencies, each serving a distinct purpose with no overlap. No heavy utility libraries, no competing HTTP clients, no duplicate-purpose packages.
The codebase consistently uses native fetch, native crypto, native path/fs operations instead of pulling in wrapper libraries. This reduces supply chain risk and bundle size.

add.header: Import cycle mitigated by dynamic imports
add.description: The extensions.ts <-> calibrate-plugin.ts cycle uses type-only imports in one direction and dynamic imports in the other, preventing runtime initialization issues while leaving a static graph artifact.

add.header: Consistent guard-check-execute-record pattern in toolkit tools
add.description: All toolkit tools follow validate-input -> check-guards -> execute-pipeline -> record-state with early returns on each step. This makes the happy path immediately visible and prevents state corruption on rejection.
The tool wrapper extracts exactly the right cross-cutting concerns (timing, touch, observer, error wrapping) in 56 lines — avoids both monolithic tools and micro-fragmented helpers.
Guards accept a boolean flag for check-only vs record mode, ensuring state is only mutated after successful pipeline execution. Precise and safe for financial operations.

All toolkit tools accept DemosSession + typed options and return ToolResult<T>. The session carries auth, bridge, policies, and state store as a single explicit boundary contract — no loose parameters crossing module boundaries.
Chain data translation happens in exactly two places: hive-codec.ts for HIVE encoding/decoding and sdk-bridge.ts for SDK transaction normalization. No scattered ad-hoc mappers.

Plugins export hook functions matching the LoopExtensionHooks interface. The immutable ExtensionHookRegistry composes them sequentially with defined short-circuit semantics. No inter-plugin communication or shared mutable state.

add.header: Legacy actions/ layer predates toolkit boundary
add.description: The actions/ layer (publish-pipeline.ts, action-executor.ts) was written before the toolkit extraction and uses direct SDK imports with its own type definitions, creating a parallel integration seam.

36 test files cover the toolkit layer including all guards, all tools, session, schemas, sdk-bridge, url-validator, types, security hardening, and integration. This is the best-tested subsystem in the codebase.
vitest globalSetup and PostToolUse hooks reject assertion-free tests, ensuring every test file contains meaningful assertions. This prevents test inflation without coverage.
Only 7 of 130 test files use snapshots (5.4%), well below the 50% overuse threshold. Tests rely on behavioral assertions rather than structural snapshots.

add.header: Consistent SDK mock pattern prevents NAPI crashes
add.description: All test files that touch SDK use vi.mock('@kynesyslabs/demosdk/websdk') with a class mock. This is necessary to prevent NAPI crashes in vitest but creates coupling to the mock structure shape.
All 10 toolkit tools share (session, opts) => ToolResult<T> with withToolWrapper providing consistent timing, error wrapping, and observer hooks. This makes the API surface predictable for consumers.

add.header: Zod validation as universal entry gate
add.description: Every tool calls validateInput(Schema, opts) as its first operation, giving consumers consistent early-fail behavior with structured error messages across all tools.

add.header: Connect uses throw semantics by design
add.description: connect/disconnect throw DemosError rather than returning ToolResult because they manage session lifecycle, not tool operations. This is documented in the connect.ts header comment and the barrel export separation.

Toolkit stores auth tokens via local Symbol on DemosSession, with toJSON() and inspect redaction. This prevents accidental leakage through logging or serialization, providing defense-in-depth beyond access control.

add.header: Uniform SSRF validation at IO boundaries
add.description: All toolkit tools that make outbound requests (attest, pay, connect) validate URLs through the same validateUrl() function with DNS resolution and IP blocklist. No tool bypasses this check.
The SdkBridge uses a 3-state apiAccess enum (none/configured/authenticated) rather than a boolean, giving precise trust boundary control for API fallback decisions throughout the codebase.

Toolkit uses session-handle auth (connect creates DemosSession), while CLI/plugins use ensureAuth() with file-cached tokens. This reflects the intentional boundary between reusable toolkit and personal CLI tooling, but means auth patterns differ across layers.

Module-level comments across toolkit, guards, and pipeline explain architectural constraints and relationships (e.g., 'Does NOT import publish-pipeline.ts to avoid TLSN/Playwright transitive deps') rather than restating what the module does. This is a deliberate documentation culture.

Only 3 pass-through wrappers out of 213 function signatures (1.4%). The codebase strongly favors direct implementation over ceremony layers, with withToolWrapper being a legitimate cross-cutting concern rather than AI-style indirection.

add.header: No defensive overengineering on typed values
add.description: Guards and tools trust their typed inputs after Zod validation — no redundant null checks on non-nullable fields, no try-catch around pure expressions. The defensive checks that exist are at genuine IO boundaries.

All toolkit tools use SDK bridge chain methods as primary path, with API as optional enrichment gated by apiAccess state. No dual-path ambiguity remains — the migration is architecturally complete, not just code-complete.

add.header: Pure TypeScript codebase with no JS residue
add.description: All 165 source files are TypeScript. var usage is down to 1 instance vs 137 let/const. No mixed JS/TS module pairs exist.

add.header: Three deprecated symbols remain as dead code
add.description: persistSourceToRegistry, RPC_URL, and SUPERCOLONY_API are deprecated and have zero callers but remain exported. These are migration cleanup items, not active confusion sources.

The src/ split into toolkit/ (reusable SDK tools), lib/ (business logic), plugins/ (extensions), reactive/ (event loop), actions/ (execution), and adapters/ (framework bridges) maps cleanly to domain capabilities. A developer can predict ownership from directory names.

toolkit/ is structured as tools/ (public API), guards/ (spending/rate controls), and root files (types, session, bridge). Coupling flows downward (tools -> guards -> types) with the barrel index.ts as the only upward reference, matching the intended package boundary.

add.header: Plugins directory is flat but cohesive
add.description: 22 plugin files in a flat directory, but all share *-plugin.ts naming and the FrameworkPlugin type contract. The high file count is appropriate because plugins are independent units that should be discoverable by scanning the directory.

13 files at the lib/ root with avg_fan_in of 1.1 represent organic growth. The existing subdirectory pattern (attestation/, pipeline/, scoring/) shows the preferred organization, but smaller feature files have not been grouped yet.

add.header: Toolkit uses factory pattern for SDK state
add.description: createSdkBridge() encapsulates all mutable state (cached modules, auth tokens) in closure scope rather than module-level globals. This is the gold standard pattern in the codebase for avoiding init-order coupling.

add.header: Lazy imports in plugins and tools
add.description: connect.ts, lifecycle-plugin.ts, and other plugins use dynamic import() to defer SDK loading. This prevents import-time side effects and keeps the module graph lightweight.

add.header: Environment reads inside functions not at module scope
add.description: process.env.AGENT_NAME, AGENT_LOG_PATH etc. are read inside function bodies (getLogAgentName, resolveAgentName), not at import time. This preserves testability and avoids fragile environment assumptions.

add.header: Observer requires explicit init before use
add.description: observe.ts has module-level mutable state (_agentName, _sessionNumber, _logPath) that must be set via initObserver() before observe() calls produce output. This is documented and intentional — the observer is best-effort and silently no-ops when uninitialized.

add.header: sdk.ts has legacy module-level mutable config
add.description: runtimeConfig, cryptoInitialized, and logAgentName are module-level let variables mutated by loadMnemonic() and setLogAgent(). This creates implicit boot-order dependencies that predate the toolkit's factory pattern.

pay-spend-cap, tip-spend-cap, write-rate-limit, and backoff all use state-helpers.ts shared utilities with typed state interfaces and atomic check-and-record operations. This is a strong data-driven structural pattern that scales well.

add.header: Plugin system has consistent hook contract
add.description: All 22 plugins follow the same FrameworkPlugin interface with typed hook contexts (BeforeSenseContext, AfterPublishDraftContext, etc.). Adding a new plugin requires no framework changes.

At 917 lines, sdk-bridge.ts mixes DAHR attestation, API calls, HIVE publishing, DEM transfers, D402 payments, and 5 chain-query methods. The chain-reader methods were added incrementally during the chain-first migration without extraction.

~1650 LOC across attestation-executor.ts, mentions.ts, predictions.ts, tips.ts, transcript.ts, and sse-feed.ts are documented orphaned files retained for future reactive loop reactivation. Not dead code — active test suites exist.

All 15+ methods on the SdkBridge interface have JSDoc describing behavior, error semantics, and return types. Return types accurately cover all code paths including null/empty cases. This is the strongest contract surface in the codebase.

add.header: Consistent throw vs result-type error strategy
add.description: Must-succeed operations (attestDahr, publishHivePost, transferDem) throw. Optional/fallback operations (apiCall, resolvePostAuthor, fetchSignals) return ok:false or null. This split is consistent and matches caller expectations at each boundary.

Observation logging is explicitly documented as best-effort and fire-and-forget. The void return type and silent failure on uninitialized state are intentional contract properties, not accidental error swallowing.

Functions across the codebase use early-return guard clauses for validation and edge cases. selectReaction, validatePayAmount, connect, and many others follow this pattern consistently, keeping happy-path logic unindented.

All pagination loops (getHivePosts, getHiveReactions, getHivePostsByAuthor) include MAX_PAGES limits, cursor advancement checks, and explicit break conditions to prevent infinite loops. This is a systematic safety pattern.
No async functions without await detected across the entire src/ tree. Every async annotation corresponds to actual asynchronous work.
calculateQualityScore uses a weights object iterated over programmatically, with MAX_SCORE computed from the weights. This makes the scoring formula self-documenting and easy to modify.

feed-filter.ts, tlsn-playwright-bridge.ts, signals.ts, engage-heuristics.ts, source-discovery.ts, and sc-oracle/sc-prices plugins have all been cleaned of explicit any types. The codebase went from high-dozens of any instances to 11 across 4 files.

add.header: DemosExtended interface replaces SDK as-any casts
add.description: tlsn-playwright-bridge.ts now uses DemosExtended and other typed interfaces instead of (demos as any) casts. This pattern was applied systematically to all 7 previous cast sites.

sdk-bridge.ts defines DemosRpcMethods with optional methods (getTxByHash?, getTransactions?) for SDK capabilities that may not exist on all versions. This avoids as-any while being honest about optional SDK surface.

add.header: z.any() used for runtime objects in schemas
add.description: schemas.ts uses z.any() for stateStore and onToolCall — runtime objects with methods that cannot be validated via Zod. Each is documented with a comment explaining the limitation. This is acceptable boundary typing.
The txHash extraction chain in publish-pipeline.ts uses 7 'as any' casts for SDK response shapes. A typed extractTxHash() function exists in sdk-bridge.ts but hasn't been adopted here yet.
